impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the defense that the tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses shields the patent holder from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court held that whether the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses was a factual question properly decided by the jury. This is significant because it reinforces that patent holders cannot leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing patent licensing demands tied to product purchase requirements may have strong antitrust counterclaims. The decision clarifies that competitors can challenge tying arrangements even where the tied product has some infringing uses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle product purchase requirements with patent licenses face substantial antitrust exposure, including damages. Companies must carefully structure licensing programs to avoid conditioning licenses on purchases of unpatented products.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are directed to an abstract idea under step one of the Alice framework for patent eligibility under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of computer security patents were directed to an abstract idea at Alice step one, requiring remand for step two analysis. The court also held that damages based on foreign sales of Norton software were improperly awarded. This decision significantly narrows patent protection for cybersecurity software methods.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing infringement suits based on software method patents gain a stronger basis to challenge such patents under § 101. The foreign sales ruling limits plaintiffs' ability to recover damages for overseas product distribution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and patent holders with computer security or software detection patents face heightened invalidity risk under Alice. The decision may reduce the value of software-related patent portfolios in the cybersecurity space.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of a patent procured by fraud.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris had acquired prior art (CYMAX Documents) describing a similar product before filing its coiled tubing patents. The court held that factual questions about whether Tenaris concealed material prior art from the PTO and whether this constituted attempted monopolization must go to trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of infringing patents potentially procured through concealment of material prior art have a viable path to both unenforceability defenses and antitrust counterclaims. The decision lowers the summary judgment bar for Walker Process claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire competitors' prior art assets before filing related patents face significant litigation risk. Both inequitable conduct defenses and treble-damage Walker Process antitrust claims may survive summary judgment where prior art concealment is alleged.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a requesting wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $2.5 million jury verdict in favor of GoTV, holding on Netflix's cross-appeal that all asserted claims of three streaming content patents are ineligible under § 101. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities. The decision required entry of judgment for Netflix and vacated the district court's rulings on inducement and damages evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies benefit from another strong precedent invalidating broadly-claimed software patents covering content delivery and device adaptation under § 101.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering content adaptation and streaming technology face increased invalidity risk. The decision further constrains patent protection for server-side content optimization methods.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket confidentiality designation for all questionnaire responses is incompatible with the statute and that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine. This decision forces the ITC to evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and domestic industries gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, enabling better access to information for litigation and compliance planning. Competitors may access previously shielded business data submitted in investigations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that submit sensitive business information in ITC questionnaire responses face potential public disclosure. Businesses participating in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations must now actively designate and justify confidential treatment for each piece of information.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants') creates a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act, giving the Court of Federal Claims jurisdiction over claims by eligible applicants who were denied grants.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed Tucker Act jurisdiction over claims by over 300 restaurants and businesses seeking unpaid RRF grants. The court held that 15 U.S.C. § 9009c's directive that the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order applications were received creates a mandatory, money-mandating obligation. The SBA's failure to award grants in the statutory order—instead processing them out of sequence—gave rise to enforceable damages claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses denied government grants under mandatory statutory programs have a viable damages remedy in the Court of Federal Claims. The decision reinforces that statutory 'shall' language creates enforceable payment obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability for hundreds of millions in unpaid RRF grants. Federal agencies administering mandatory grant programs face increased litigation risk when they deviate from statutory processing requirements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft in a patent case involving network optimization patents. The court found the district court applied an overly rigid standard to the expert's methodology and improperly granted summary judgment on the sole ground that Exafer lacked admissible damages evidence. This decision provides important guidance on the interplay between Daubert challenges and patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more flexible standard for admissibility of damages expert testimony. The decision limits defendants' ability to obtain dispositive relief solely by excluding the plaintiff's damages case.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending against patent suits face reduced ability to eliminate cases at summary judgment through Daubert motions targeting damages experts.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding plaintiff's technical and damages experts and granting JMOL of non-infringement in a medical device patent case involving induced infringement by surgeons.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and its grant of JMOL for DePuy in a case involving spinal surgery derotation device patents. The court found the exclusions were an abuse of discretion and that, with the experts' testimony, sufficient evidence existed for a jury to find induced infringement. The case was remanded for a new trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space benefit from a more liberal standard for expert testimony on induced infringement theories involving physician end-users.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device manufacturers face renewed induced infringement exposure where their products can be configured by physicians in allegedly infringing ways. Expert exclusion strategies may be less effective as a defense.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's ruling that the PTO Director's instructions governing IPR institution discretion are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions even after Board action. This validates the PTO's ability to set IPR institution policies without formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued discretion to deny IPR petitions under its existing framework without being constrained by rulemaking requirements.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies that rely on IPR to challenge patents face continued uncertainty from discretionary denials, with no procedural mechanism to compel notice-and-comment rulemaking on institution policies.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered only a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) rather than the general exclusion order sought by the trademark holder, and whether the appeal of the no-violation finding against active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely, and affirmed the limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The ITC had found no likelihood of confusion or dilution of Crocs's 3D trademark registrations for its Classic Clog. The decision clarifies that under § 1337(g)(1), the ITC is required to issue exclusion orders against defaulting parties but retains discretion over the scope of relief.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners can still obtain exclusion orders against defaulting importers in Section 337 proceedings even where the ITC finds no violation by active respondents. Default provides a presumption of the complaint's allegations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad general exclusion orders face difficulty when active respondents successfully defend against trademark claims. The strict appeal deadline means companies must carefully calendar filing requirements in ITC proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a means-plus-function 'control means' limitation in a patent claiming a crop harvester height apparatus is indefinite when the specification describes the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were invalid as indefinite. The court held that the specification's description of a controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system as corresponding structure was sufficiently definite, even though the patent described elements as 'conventional.' The case was remanded for further proceedings on infringement against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more permissive standard for corresponding structure disclosure, particularly where specifications describe well-known components as 'conventional.'
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies like Deere defending against means-plus-function claims face reduced ability to invalidate patents for indefiniteness where specifications reference conventional technology without full structural detail.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 who receive Title 38 base pay rates are also entitled to Title 38 enhanced compensation for overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday work.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 and Title 5 regulations. The court held that the nurses adequately stated claims that they were denied compensation enhancements available to Title 5 healthcare employees who receive Title 38 base pay. The decision potentially affects all similarly situated current and former IHS registered nurses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential backpay liability for failing to provide enhanced compensation rates to nurses receiving Title 38 base pay. The class action nature of the claims could result in significant aggregate liability for the government.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of data streams are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement action against Google, holding that web conferencing patent claims enabling time-shifted, asynchronous observation of multimedia presentations are patent-ineligible under § 101. The decision continues the trend of invalidating patents claiming broadly-described software functionality for web-based communication and collaboration tools.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and collaboration platforms gain protection against patent assertions based on broad functional claims to time-shifting and asynchronous viewing capabilities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering web conferencing and multimedia collaboration features face heightened § 101 invalidity risk at the pleading stage.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether substantial evidence supported the jury's finding of nonobviousness and whether the damages award for a patent covering pre-lit artificial tree trunk connectors was supported by the evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL on both obviousness and damages for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical-electrical trunk connectors. The court held the jury's nonobviousness finding was supported by substantial evidence and that the damages methodology was sound. The decision upholds significant patent protection in the consumer products space for mechanical-electrical integration innovations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Consumer product patent holders benefit from strong deference to jury findings on obviousness and damages, particularly where the patent covers a practical integration improvement over prior art requiring separate connections.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the consumer products space face continued patent enforcement risk where patents claim practical mechanical improvements, even in seemingly simple product categories.
